data.title: DouYu: Worth The Risk At Far Below Cash On The Books
data.description:
DouYu (DOYU), a live streaming platform in China for games and e-sports, is off to a horrible 2026 with the stock having lost 31.5% YTD, and that is with a bounce after the release of the Q4 FY2025 report on March 25. However, DOYU has a shot at adding to the recent bounce for a number of reasons, especially if DOYU can maintain what it showed in the latest report.

DOYU has sold off, but a bullish signal may herald better times

A previous article from November 2025 written by myself mentioned a number of issues that needed to be addressed by DOYU, which included declining revenue and a decrease in monthly active users, or MAUs, both direct consequences of a downturn in the market for live streaming in China. On the other hand, DOYU was working on addressing its faults and making some progress in certain areas, which gave reason for optimism that DOYU could fix whatever that needed to be addressed. Accordingly, I chose to rate DOYU a buy in the article.

For a while it looked like DOYU had managed to stop the large decline in the stock that started in July 2025, as it headed into the new year having gone sideways for several months, but 2026 saw a resumption of the decline in the stock. The chart above shows how the stock fell in the first couple of months to hit a 2026 low of $4.28 per ADS on February 24, although it closed the day significantly higher at $4.48.

However, what follows next could be viewed as a bullish signal for longs. Note how in the chart above, the bounce following the February 24 low was succeeded by another move downwards that resulted in DOYU almost matching the February 24 low on March 24. The stock hit an intraday low of $4.50 per ADS on March 24, but closed the day somewhat higher at $4.53. All this is significant for multiple reasons.

First, the lows are exactly one month apart. On a closing basis, the February 24 low of $4.48 and the March 24 low of $4.53, are just $0.05 apart, or roughly one percent of each other. The fact that the March 24 low is somewhat higher is also worth noting. All this points in the direction of a double bottom, which is considered a bullish reversal chart pattern, especially since it follows in the wake of a long decline that started with the July 2025 high of $9.34 per ADS.

If this is correct, and the stock is heading north after reversing course, then DOYU may be able to move unopposed until the first potential resistance level. This could be the 23.6% Fibonacci retracement of the preceding move downwards, or the July 2025 high of $9.34 to the February 2026 low of $4.28, which is $5.47.

If DOYU manages to get past the first hurdle at $5.47, DOYU may be able to go as high as the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement level at $7.41 per ADS, before resistance is likely to offer its strongest challenge to prevent DOYU from moving higher. But to get there, DOYU would first need to get past the 38.2% and 50% Fibonacci retracement levels, which are at $6.21 and $6.81, respectively.

What could drive DOYU higher

While DOYU may have sent a bullish signal, that alone may not be enough to convince everyone that DOYU is worth pursuing. Some may need more to get on board. It just so happens that there are other catalysts that could power a move higher by DOYU. But before we go into that, it is necessary to look at what caused DOYU to fall off in recent years, since that would help explain how it can get out of the hole.

DOYU is a live streaming platform in China, which greatly benefited when COVID-19 broke out and the government in China resorted to lockdowns. This is because it removed a number of potential avenues for entertainment, especially those that required large numbers of people in close proximity to each other, which opened opportunities for live streaming. More people would turn to live streaming because it was compatible with people staying in the confines of their homes during the pandemic.

DOYU grew greatly during the pandemic years, but the reverse happened when the lockdowns were lifted and people could go back to doing all the things they used to do before the pandemic. Live streaming saw a reduction in the number of users and/or the time spent on the platform, which naturally affected DOYU. A slower economy meant people were less willing to spend. Adding to the pressure were new regulations in China that sought to combat some of the problems on live streaming platforms.

This included, for instance, some influencers using the live streaming platform to make misleading or even false claims in order to promote certain products and/or services. China has also sought to regulate the practice of tipping or other forms of rewards during live streaming. In short, action was taken to curb various issues on live streaming platforms that were deemed problematic, all of which impacted DOYU.

DOYU seems to be recovering, according to the latest report

The live streaming industry underwent a downturn as a result of the above and other reasons, which also made its way to DOYU. DOYU saw both the top and the bottom line shrink compared to the heady days of the pandemic around 2021 or so. However, recent reports show DOYU is recovering as quarterly results have significantly improved.

In the most recent or Q4 FY2025 report, released on March 25, FY2025 revenue decreased to RMB3,818.85M, or $546.09M, less than RMB4,270.83M in FY2024, but DOYU was able to reduce its GAAP net loss attributable to shareholders to RMB29.08M, or $4.16M, which is much less than the net loss of RMB306.81M a year ago.

The results look even better when only the core business is considered. Income from operations actually got out of the red with RMB4.76M, or $0.68M, in FY2025, better than the loss of RMB573.59M in FY2024. If not for impairment charges, which pushed the net results into the red, the numbers could have looked even better.

Per share, the loss of RMB0.96 or $0.14 per ADS in FY2025 is better than the loss of RMB9.95 in FY2024. In terms of non-GAAP, which leaves out a number of items like impairment charges, DOYU reported net income of RMB40.16M, or $5.74M, in FY2025, better than the net loss of RMB249.23M in FY2024. This translates to a net income of RMB1.33 or $0.19 per ADS in FY2025, up from a net loss of RMB8.08 in FY2024.

Long DOYU can count on a strong balance sheet

The improvement in the income statement has implications for the balance sheet. DOYU has a very strong balance sheet, but as long as the income statement is in the red, one has to account for the possibility the balance sheet will be negatively affected by the income statement. But if the income statement is no longer a problem, or something close to it, then that lifts a weight off of the balance sheet.

This is important because DOYU has a low valuation in comparison to its balance sheet. DOYU ended FY2025 with cash and cash equivalents of $251.55M, which by itself far exceeds DOYU’s current market cap of $141M. There is also another $71.86M in short-term bank deposits, which means DOYU is valued at less than half the net cash, or what can be converted to cash, on the books since total debt stands at just $1.4M. This does not include another $54.87M DOYU has in long-term investments.

As far as the balance sheet is concerned, DOYU has a book value of $9.33 per ADS, which means DOYU is valued at a price-to-book of just 0.5x, with the stock last closing at $4.66 per ADS on March 27. If we assume DOYU will no longer spend time in the red since both operating income and non-GAAP net income were positive and GAAP income would have been if not for impairment charges, then DOYU could potentially be worth twice what it currently goes for.

What DOYU needs to do to clear doubts

However, if DOYU wants to get back to what it is supposed to be worth according to book value, if not higher, it needs to remove all doubts that it is at risk of incurring net losses since that can reduce book value if left to its devices. DOYU did show progress by getting out of the red in terms of most metrics, but it is still quite close to falling back into the red to take it easy.

For instance, income from operations turned positive after eliminating the big losses from before, but at just $0.68M, it would not take much for DOYU to fall back into the red. The main issue for DOYU is that the top line continues to shrink, even though the bottom line has gotten better. While DOYU managed to end in the black in pretty much everything in the last or Q4 FY2025 quarter, including non-GAAP and GAAP net income and operating income, revenue still contracted.

In Q4 FY2025, revenue decreased from RMB1,136.0M a year ago to RMB918.78M, or $131.38M, even though it reported income from operations of $675,000, GAAP net income of $196,000 or $0.01 per ADS, and non-GAAP net income of $1.8M, or $0.06 per ADS, all much better than one year ago. This continued contraction could create doubt in the minds of potential buyers, as it leaves open the possibility DOYU could regress and fall back into the red in terms of the income statement. DOYU will need to show it can grow revenue sustainably if it does not want to be discounted based on its balance sheet.

The silver lining here is that DOYU may be doing better in terms of revenue than the headline numbers suggest it is. DOYU has become more selective in terms of what live streaming events to host by focusing less on those where returns are lower in terms of profitability. While these events may bring in revenue, they add little to the bottom line and may even be subtracting from it.

Eliminating them helps DOYU’s profitability, but it also means less revenue coming in. The flip side is that once DOYU has weeded out all the low performers, the negative impact on the top line should go away. Once this happens, revenue growth could return, which would work well with an improved bottom line.

Investor takeaways

DOYU has gone through a period in which the company struggled to adapt to a new environment after the boom during the pandemic. However, there are signs DOYU is getting back on track. Income from operations turned positive in FY2025, as did non-GAAP net income. In terms of GAAP, DOYU was still in the red, although impairment charges were a factor, but it is good to see how DOYU managed to improve on this in the final or Q4 FY2025 quarterly report with a GAAP profit. All in all, the recently released report is consistent with a company on the upswing.

This is relevant in relation to the balance sheet. DOYU is currently available at less than half the net cash, or what can be converted to cash, on the books. This could be justified if there was reason to believe book value could go down due to net losses, but this argument becomes harder to make if the income statement is improving to the point there are no more losses.

The case for long DOYU has another argument in its favor with the stock posting what looks like a double bottom chart pattern. This could signal a reversal in the stock, which suggests the stock is heading higher after going down for some time. Combine it with what is going on in the income statement and the balance sheet, and long DOYU is at the very least not without some merit.

DOYU does need to show it can grow the top line, but while the headline numbers have yet to show this, a closer look at what is going on at DOYU gives reason to believe that this too is something DOYU can achieve. In any case, when valuations are so low that DOYU is valued at less than half net cash, it becomes easier to forgive DOYU for a flaw it has yet to get rid of.

I am thus bullish on DOYU all things considered. There is the risk that the bottom line could regress due to a contraction in the top line, but at less than half the net cash, DOYU is worth the risk. DOYU has been able to greatly improve the most pressing issue, profitability, and it deserves a shot at showing it can solve the other problem of lower revenue. If DOYU can be considered a profitable business, then it should not be worth much less than the cash it has access to, like it is right now. Chances are this won’t remain the case for long.

data.title: Buda Juice: The Optionality Isn't Mine
data.description:
Some say Buddha spent 42 whole days meditating under the Bodhi tree. Well, my coverage of Buda Juice (BUDA) is a little over that. About three months.

But, to tell the truth, my 'Sell' call here hasn't reached enlightenment yet. It delivered ~4% while the S&P fell just over 7%.

Funny that I followed the post-earnings reaction. It wasn't good at all. I don't know if IPO investors expected faster post-IPO growth or something like that.

The stock started to slip in the high single digits, but recovered in the middle of the session and went flat.

The pre-market view isn't very pretty.

Right now it's down ~5%. You know how things are. In a stock with such low float, that means practically nothing.

Just as a reminder, the Buda Juice thesis is really about the scale Country Fresh (Brian Herr's company and Buda's second-largest shareholder) could bring. It gains access to proprietary UltraFresh tech, while Buda taps into nationwide distribution.

It's very good for Herr, but I have my doubts about whether it's really good for the minority shareholder at these multiples.

You would be paying ~8x sales for the acceleration of the distribution curve (Country Fresh will accelerate this) and, consequently, the risk of executing the expansion. But, looking at the other side, you would also be paying for an optionality that isn't yours.

Who will guarantee that there won't be value leakage for the minority shareholder?

Buda Juice's CEO sold some shares in the IPO, and if Herr becomes the majority shareholder, he could (and it would even be the most understandable thing to do) prioritize Country Fresh.

There are many 'whys' on the table. And, to keep things realistic, I'll give a peek at the quarter.

Nothing New Under The Sun

To start off, if I'm basing this on the material management provided to us at the IPO, Buda has a 38,000 sq ft production plant.

And that was expected. The proceeds from the IPO (~$20 million) are expected to be allocated to two new projects (one in South Carolina and another expanding the existing one on the West Coast).

The Dallas plant is probably already running. This would give them a maximum revenue capacity of $55 million today.

As they pulled $12.6 million in all of FY 2025, I imagine they are operating at only 22.9% of their current production capacity.

Well, I expected something like that.

In fact, I even expected a slightly bigger bump. My projections were targeting $19.5 million in the base scenario.

Regarding capacity, I also imagined something similar. Buda juices (like all organic juices) have a short shelf life. This is a model where you simply can't have overcapacity if you don't want to see your inventory spoiled.

Did the market want to see stronger growth this quarter? I really don't know, but I suspect so, given the post-earnings reaction.

Gross margin also contracted slightly. It fell from 46.2% to 44.4%.

It's not something you should worry too much about, as the CEO told us it was due to some increase in the price of citrus fruits. I guess I should get used to stable/slightly declining gross margins for FY 2026/2027.

Add to that higher SG&A due to the IPO, and you'll see a slightly lower EBITDA margin as well. As I said, it's nothing to worry about.

But right now it doesn't look like a company that should trade at 8x sales.

Here's a peek at the margins:

The good news is that they remain larger than peers like Vita Coco (COCO) and should maintain their structural size as they expand.

Of course, there's the typical problem of any cold chain when expanding. But, at the level I'm seeing here, the EBIT margin would still be quite healthy. Horatio Lonsdale-Hands, the CEO of Buda Juice, rightly likes to say that this is a profitable small-cap since its IPO.

If I earned a dollar for every company that's just another 'empty promise' during its IPO, I'd be rich by now. That's not the case here.

Buda generated $3.2 million in OCF in the quarter. Almost all of that ($3 million, to be more precise) became FCF given the very low Capex. It's still an FCF yield of just over 2.3%, but the idea is that this will increase as they execute the plan.

$55 Billion? I Don't Think So

Taking advantage of the fact that Buda Juice is a company that sells healthy products, it's time for me to try and ask some healthy questions.

The first one has to do with the TAM thesis they are aiming for going forward.

For example, the company's materials estimate a market of ~$55 billion in 2024 with no 'true fresh juice on the market.' By 2033 they expect an even larger market ($77.5 billion) and the 'true fresh juice leader' throne vacant.

From what I understand, the idea is for Buda Juice to reclaim this throne.

But I think this runs into some exaggerations.

The first is that there is no other 'true fresh' juice and that consumers would really see Buda Juice as part of a category apart from other juices out there.

Most folks will just buy the fruit and juice it at home.

Demand here depends on both Gen-Z and health-conscious Millennials. And, taking a peek at consumer confidence, you can see that despite these being the most confident generations, they seem to be cutting back on fitness expenses here and there.

It seems to me that the achievable TAM is lower. I won't guess a specific number, but I imagine something up to ~50% lower and perhaps a bit volatile since there's built-in elasticity here.

Adjusting Some Projections

For Q1 FY 2026, the CFO told us on the earnings call that revenue was growing at mid-teens year-over-year.

So, I'm aiming for something close to $3.8 to $3.9 million in revenue. I know it's quite difficult, but if we annualize that growth, FY 2026 would deliver a total revenue of almost $15.5 million.

But there are some caveats here.

The table I showed you earlier shows a total capacity of $110 million after the East Coast plant opens. That's 14% utilization of total capacity. I'll use this as a possible base scenario.

As a bull, I can project that they maintain current utilization (22.9%) and reach $25.2 million. Almost 100% year-over-year growth.

It seems risky, but perhaps a ramp-up from Country Fresh could deliver that right away.

If FY 2026 is a mystery, FY 2027 is even more.

If we follow what the chart actually showed, capacity at the end of the year would reach $165 million, ~$37.8 million based on current capacity utilization.

Will it be possible to deliver a CAGR of over 30%? Well, again, I think so, but only if Country Fresh does the heavy lifting.

But maybe I'm being too conservative using that capacity, right? I'll stick with my FY 2026 scenarios, and I want you to tell me your predictions in the comments.

The more viewpoints, the better.

Now, regarding the EBITDA margin, I'm going to consider it slightly lower. Here I'm modeling the impact of slightly more expensive citrus fruits and some inefficiencies when they expand the cold chain to the East Coast.

So, if they pulled ~29%, I imagine something closer to 27%.

We're talking about an EBITDA of $4.1 to $6.8 million. It's a huge range, but it's justifiable given all the uncertainty here.

Taking Vita Coco's EV/EBITDA (~21.8x) and considering that $5 of the $20 million they scooped up in the IPO will be used for the East Coast plant, I arrive at a price target of $6.50 to $10.70 for the base and bull scenarios, respectively.

Remember that for the bull scenario to happen, they would need to double their revenue by FY 2026.

If I take a weighted average where this would have a ~20% chance of happening, the price target here would be just over $7.50.

Pretty close to what I found in the last article, don't you think?

The best thing to do (in my view, of course) is to stay out of this IPO and observe from the sidelines.

Sales growing in the low teens isn't what minority investors would like to see with the Dallas plant already operational. I imagine this could start scaling at the end of this year when the East Coast plant opens.

With these fat margins, a soaring revenue (remember that for upside above $10 per share it would almost need to double) would be a good trigger for a 'Buy' here.

Buda Juice still seems to me more like a cold chain company, which is a great driver for Country Fresh. Anything beyond that is quite speculative, and, precisely because it's a well-managed company, it's trading at sky-high multiples.

data.title: Whale's Insight: A Macro-Driven Market With No Safe Haven, And No End To Volatility
data.description:
This week, Trump's flip-flopping triggered three major market reversals in five days as gold, equities, and crypto fell in unison with no safe haven in sight. Multiple scenarios are taking shape depending on how long the Hormuz closure lasts and where the conflict heads. On-chain commodity perpetuals are booming as tradfi migrates to 24/7 venues, while institutional ETF flows are fading on both BTC (BTC-USD) and ETH (ETH-USD).

Three Scenarios for a Macro-Driven, Volatile Market

Trump's Flip-Flopping Is the Largest Source of Market Volatility

This week, Trump's media posts triggered three major directional reversals across global markets in under five days, producing over $1 billion in crypto liquidations alone.

  • Mar 22 (Sat): Trump demands Iran reopen the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours or face destruction of its power plants. BTC drops over the weekend. $400M+ in crypto futures liquidated, 85% longs.

  • Mar 23 (Sun): Iran rejects the ultimatum, threatens to destroy Gulf energy infrastructure. Oil futures gap higher; Brent briefly touches $119.

  • Mar 24 (Mon): Trump TACOs. Announces a 5-day pause on strikes, citing "productive conversations" with Tehran. Markets explode in the opposite direction. Oil crashes 14% and S&P futures swing from -1% to +3%. This time it is the shorts getting wiped: $383M in crypto shorts liquidated in one hour.

  • Mar 26 (Thu): Conflict re-escalates. Oil surges (Brent +5.7%), and the S&P drops 1.74%, its worst single day of 2026. After market close, Trump extends the deadline again to April 6. Second reversal in five days.

BTC was whipsawed between $67K and $71K, a $4,000 range driven entirely by geopolitical headlines. The violent wicks in both directions produced over $1 billion in total cryptocurrency liquidations across the week.

Hormuz Scenarios: What Happens If This Drags On

The real substance behind all the market volatility is this: roughly 20% of global oil and gas transits through the Strait of Hormuz, and that waterway has been effectively shut for four weeks.

The IEA chief called the current situation worse than the combined 1973 and 1979 oil crises.

We currently sit between "partial disruption" and "full closure, 30 days." Trump's April 6 extension guarantees at least 10 more days of uncertainty.

If Hormuz stays shut past mid-April, the market reprices toward $150+ oil and a global recession scenario.

Cross-Asset Fallout

Crypto is being driven entirely by macro. BTC sits at $69–70K, down 46% from its ATH, and the broader crypto market is down 21% YTD. There are no crypto-native catalysts for the decline.

The transmission chain is clear: oil spike → inflation expectations rise → Fed stays hawkish → dollar strengthens → all non-yielding risk assets get sold.

The derivatives market confirms the bearish positioning: put/call open interest ratio has reached 0.84, the highest since June 2021; funding rates are negative across most altcoins; and CryptoQuant warns that structural bottom signals have not appeared.

When a market ignores all good news, it is telling you that the macro overhang must clear before anything else can matter.

Gold has fallen 22% from its $5,602 ATH to $4,373, with last week's 9.6% drop the worst since 2011. Nine consecutive down days.

Gold crashing during a war is the most counterintuitive signal of this cycle. It tells you the market fears the inflation-to-rate-hike-to-recession chain reaction more than the war itself.

S&P 500 posted its worst day of 2026 on March 26 (-1.74%). Russell 2000 entered correction territory on March 20. Four consecutive weekly declines. Energy is the only sector in the green.

Beneficiaries: Oil, energy equities, tanker stocks, defense stocks, and fertilizer producers. The U.S. dollar is strengthening as the sole safe haven, punishing all EM currencies and net energy importers.

What Comes Next: Scenarios That Will Shape Every Asset

March 27 presents a rare catalyst convergence on a single day:

  1. U.S. February Core PCE release, the Fed's preferred inflation measure. Early estimates point to a hot print, with Capital Economics projecting core PCE at 0.42% MoM, which would keep the YoY rate pinned near 3.1%, well above the Fed's 2% target. This number will determine whether the "higher for longer" narrative strengthens further or begins to soften.

  2. A $14.16B Bitcoin options expiry on Deribit, with max pain at $75K, roughly 8% above current spot price.

Beyond today, the trajectory of markets depends on two variables above all else: the duration of the Hormuz closure and the direction of the war. Below are the three most probable scenarios and their cross-asset implications.

Scenario A: De-escalation Within Weeks (TACO Base Case)

Trump reaches some form of face-saving deal before or around the April 6 deadline. Hormuz reopens partially. Oil falls back toward $85–90.

  • Crypto: Sharp relief rally. BTC likely retests $75K–80K as the macro overhang lifts and ETF inflows resume at pre-FOMC pace. Altcoins with strong fundamentals (SOL, ETH) could see outsized bounces given how compressed positioning is.

  • Gold: Rebounds toward $4,800–5,000 as real rate pressure eases and the structural bull case (central bank buying, de-dollarization) reasserts.

  • Equities: Quick recovery. Russell 2000 exits correction territory. Tech leads. Defense and energy give back gains.

Scenario B: Prolonged Stalemate (1–3 Months)

No deal. Hormuz remains partially disrupted. Oil grinds between $100 and $150. Military operations continue at current intensity.

  • Crypto: Continues trading as a macro hostage. BTC range-bound between $60K and $72K, with periodic wicks driven by Trump posts and war headlines. Institutional ETF flows stay mixed and low-conviction.

  • Gold: Finds a floor in the $4,200-4,500 range as paper liquidation exhausts itself, then slowly recovers. Physical premiums remain elevated.

  • Equities: Grind lower. Recession probability rises. The Atlanta Fed GDPNow, already at 2.3%, could approach zero or turn negative. EM equities under severe pressure from dollar strength and energy costs.

Scenario C: Full Escalation (Tail Risk)

Hormuz shuts completely for 3+ months. The conflict widens (Hezbollah, Houthis). Oil surges past $200. Central banks are paralyzed.

  • Crypto: COVID-style broad risk-off. BTC could retest $50K–55K as institutional capital exits all risk assets. However, ETF structural demand and the $70K short-term holder cost basis provide a harder floor than previous cycles. A capitulation flush followed by aggressive institutional re-entry is the pattern to watch.

  • Gold: After the initial paper liquidation phase, gold re-emerges as the ultimate safe haven. A move toward $6,000+ becomes plausible as fiat confidence erodes.

  • Equities: Bear market. S&P 500 decline of 20%+ from current levels. Energy and defense are the only hedges. Dollar strengthens aggressively, crushing EM currencies and triggering capital flight.

The Variable That Matters Most

Duration is everything. A 2-week resolution is a buying opportunity across every asset class. A 6-month closure rewrites the macro playbook entirely. The market cannot price this binary outcome, which is exactly why volatility remains extreme.

Trump's April 6 deadline provides an uncertain window of calm, but ground-level military escalation continues regardless of diplomatic signals. U.S.-Iran peace talks remain highly uncertain. As long as neither side can reach a compromise that aligns their core interests, the conflict will continue to drain markets. That said, Trump could TACO at any moment, and the Strait of Hormuz could reopen just as suddenly.

In a market defined by this level of unpredictability, investors should approach every position with caution.

Commodities Now Dominate Perpetual Futures

Traditional financial assets are increasingly being traded on-chain through perpetual futures, and the growth is accelerating. Aggregate open interest across permissionless perpetual markets reached an all-time high of $1.74 billion on March 23, up 25% in a single week.

Commodity-linked perpetuals now account for over 67% of permissionless perpetual futures activity in Q1 2026, up from near zero just six months ago.

On March 23, on-chain commodity perpetual markets hit a record single-day volume of $5.4 billion. The breakdown: silver $1.3B, WTI crude $1.2B, Brent crude $940M and gold $558M. Oil and silver perps generated more volume than SOL and XRP combined that day.

The chart below shows HIP-3 markets by asset category.

Oil-linked perpetual contracts have hit $1.7 billion in peak daily volume with roughly $300 million in open interest. The driver is simple: when CME closes for the weekend, on-chain oil perps are the only venue for real-time price discovery.

Weekend trading activity has surged 9x since January. The chart below shows HIP-3 weekend trading volume (USD).

Macro risk no longer waits for market hours. On-chain perpetuals have emerged as a 24/7 global barometer for geopolitical and commodity risk, blurring the line between crypto infrastructure and traditional derivatives markets.

Market Flow: Institutional Money Is Hesitating

BTC Spot ETF: Four consecutive weeks of net inflows have been broken. The FOMC's hawkish signal on March 18, combined with escalating war uncertainty, killed the momentum that had been building since early March.

ETH Spot ETF: The picture is worse. Not only was the six-day inflow streak terminated, but flows have been net negative since March 18, draining approximately $1.1 billion in total.

Institutional capital is hesitating again. The combination of a hawkish Fed, surging oil, and unpredictable war headlines has pushed institutions back to the sidelines.

ETF inflows have been the only structural source of buying since January. When that flow dries up, there is simply no incremental capital entering the market in the short term.

Week Ahead

  • Ongoing: Iran war enters Week 5. The Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed. Ground-level escalation is now the primary focus, and any ceasefire signal could end the conflict rapidly.

  • Mar 31: Eurozone Core CPI Flash Estimate (YoY)

  • Apr 1: US ISM Manufacturing PMI (March)

  • Apr 6: Trump's extended deadline for Iran to reach a deal or face strikes on energy infrastructure

The war and the April 6 deadline define the window. Whether Trump TACOs again, escalates, or reaches an actual deal will set the direction for oil, and oil will set the direction for everything else.

Meanwhile, the CPI and PMI releases will show whether the oil shock is already feeding into real economic damage. If European inflation runs hot and U.S. manufacturing contracts, the stagflation narrative hardens further, leaving central banks with even less room to act.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

data.title: Plug Power: Some Progress But Major Headwinds Remain - Sell
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Plug Power Inc., or “Plug Power” (PLUG), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Earlier this month, Plug Power reported better-than-expected fourth-quarter results, with both sales and profitability coming in ahead of consensus estimates:

Net sales of $225.2 million represented a new multi-year high for the company.

However, outperformance was almost solely the result of an unexpected increase in service parts sales, which caused service revenue to more than double on a sequential basis.

For my part, I would consider this more of a one-time benefit and firmly expect service revenues to come down substantially in Q1.

Sales also benefited from the recognition of long-delayed electrolyzer revenues. However, the company failed to replace these deployments with new orders, thus resulting in electrolyzer backlog dropping to new multi-year lows:

More importantly, Plug Power made substantial progress on the margin front, with product revenues finally approaching break-even levels.

Please note that major sequential improvements in the service and leasing segment appear to be mostly the result of the above-discussed parts sales and, based on disclosures made in the company's annual report on Form 10-K, a "cumulative catch-up adjustment" resulting from the modification of a leasing agreement with an undisclosed customer.

But even without these sizeable one-time benefits, Plug Power has made progress on the service front in recent quarters. In the 10-K, the company lists several reasons for the improvements:

  • pricing adjustments
  • improved stack reliability
  • reduced part costs
  • increased labor utilization

As a result, the company decided to release a whopping $18.3 million from its previously established provision for money-losing service contracts. Without the release, service gross margins would have remained in negative territory:

The combination of one-time benefits and tangible business improvements resulted in consolidated GAAP gross margin turning positive for the first time in recent years.

Adjusted for the accounting impact of warrants issued to key customers Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT), consolidated gross margin improved from 2.4% to 8.9%:

Unfortunately, cash usage remains significant. During the fourth quarter, the company burned approximately $170 million:

Without an almost $60 million benefit from the release of restricted cash, cash usage would have been even higher:

Despite persistent cash burn, Plug Power's unrestricted cash balances more than doubled on a quarter-over-quarter basis to $368.5 million, mostly due to $355.1 million in net proceeds received from a highly dilutive warrant inducement agreement.

Management expects to increase liquidity by another $275+ million from the monetization of electricity rights after abandoning plans to complete or start construction of additional green hydrogen plants with funds provided under a $1.66 billion DOE loan guarantee.

The announcement marked the official end to Plug Power's ambitious green hydrogen production plans with a targeted capacity of 1,000 tons per day (tpd) by 2028.

In recent years, the company has wasted billions of capex dollars projecting and constructing these plants, but after many delays and substantial cost overruns, only a small 15 tpd facility in Georgia was completed.

Last month, the company entered into a definitive agreement with Stream US Data Centers, LLC pursuant to which Plug Power agreed to sell the site and associated electrical infrastructure of its abandoned green hydrogen plant in Genesee County, New York, for a purchase price ranging between $132.5 million and $142.0 million.

On the conference call, management provided directional guidance for 2026:

  • revenue growth to be "directionally comparable" to 2025 which translates into projected sales of approximately $800 million, driven primarily by the company's material handling and electrolyzer operations. Please note that this guidance was well below consensus expectations at that time.
  • The company is expected to achieve positive EBITDAs in the fourth quarter.
  • Continued improvement in cash usage "similar to the reduction achieved in 2025" or approximately $400 million.

In combination with the above-discussed sales proceeds, cash on hand should be sufficient to avoid additional shareholder dilution.

Looking further ahead, management projected positive operating income for 2027 and GAAP profitability in 2028.

However, given the company's track record, investors should take these projections with a huge grain of salt.

Please note also that management's growth expectations are not supported by Plug Power's backlog:

While up slightly on a sequential basis, backlog of $724.1 million was down by almost 20% on a year-over-year basis. Absent major build-to-order business, 2026 revenues are not likely to be anywhere close to the $800 million number envisioned by management.

In addition, with electrolyzer backlog at multi-year lows, management's stated expectation for the business to be a major growth driver this year makes little sense.

Lastly, investors should not get carried away by the company's perceived data center opportunity, as Plug Power's PEM fuel cells require expensive hydrogen, thus making them unsuitable for providing primary power at scale.

In sum, while the company has been making some tangible progress in recent quarters, cash burn remains high, and stated growth expectations do not add up with Plug Power's declining backlog.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on the company's shares.

Risk Factors

As usual, the bear thesis is not without risks, particularly in today's hype-driven market.

Should the company finally start delivering on its promises, market participants would likely start to rerate the stock.

Bottom Line

Plug Power reported better-than-expected fourth quarter results. While the quarter benefited from a number of one-time gains, the company has made tangible progress on the margin front as of late.

For 2026, management guided for continued sales growth and decreased cash usage, very similar to last year. Even more importantly, Plug Power expects to achieve positive EBITDAs in the fourth quarter, with additional improvement expected in 2027 followed by GAAP profitability in 2028.

Unfortunately, cash burn remains high, and management's stated growth expectations are not supported by current backlog levels.

As a result, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on the company's shares.

data.title: Mach Natural Resources: Wait For A Full Business Cycle Record
data.description:
Mach Natural Resources LP (MNR) is an upstream partnership that issues a K-1. Typically, this combination does not work well throughout the business cycle. Back when I first began writing for Seeking Alpha, there were various income models that several upstream companies had set up (including K-1s). None of those models are around now.

The reason for this seems to be the big oil price decline that occurred in 2015 followed by the COVID challenges in 2020. The two of those events seem to have proven that the idea did not work. It should also be pointed out that 2008 was no picnic for this type of setup either.

Upstream is very dependent upon commodity prices. Low commodity prices do offer the potential of reported income on a K-1 without getting a distribution. That means that you have to pay taxes without getting the cash to do so. K-1 partnerships do much better with a midstream setup where the income is much steadier (and reliable) with some downside protection.

The other issue is the calculation of disposable cash is fixed. This often allows for a generous distribution during a time when commodity prices are elevated (such as oil prices right now). But the company is reporting a debt ratio of 1.4 currently. There is a target of a debt ratio of 1.0. The problem with this in the past has been that a significant downturn turned a comfortable debt ratio into something nearly impossible to service. This is where a lot of these kinds of K-1s vanished for any number of reasons.

This company, on the other hand, went public in 2023.

Since going public, the stock has clearly dropped about 50% from its high point and rebounded a bit on the Iranian situation that has raised commodity prices. However, most K-1s are buying considerations at market bottoms when commodity prices are weak. The current situation is the polar opposite of a buy consideration. This is probably the time to consider selling because “the sun is shining and the forecast is for sunny skies well into the future.” Except that this is a low visibility industry that can quickly change environments with little warning.

The issue for me is that the debt is not the priority that the distribution to unit holders is. In my opinion, what should be happening right now is a paydown of the debt (as fast as possible) to a very low debt ratio so that the debt can still be properly serviced if the worst happens. It is noted in the 10-K that debt can become a priority. But usually when that happens, it is too late. There is insufficient cash flow at that time (usually a cyclical downturn near the bottom) to deal with the situation. If the entity survives in any form, usually there is a lot of dilution and a complete change to a corporation going forward.

The last issue is that this entity is operating in some interesting basins that are generally not considered low cost even though they are viable basins. There is every chance the seller wanted out of the properties because the seller had gone through the latest natural gas pricing downturn (and had enough).

Areas Of Operation

Probably the San Juan Basin serves as a good example:

The San Juan Basin Royalty Trust (SJT) has struggled for some time. It was a basin that had a fairly long period of inactivity before the trust reported drilling again on the expectation of stronger natural gas prices. But the trust has also not issued a distribution for some time because it has considerable loans (and debts) to repay. In fact, there is a serious possibility that it may have to liquidate because it is getting near the end of its financial rope.

Because Mach formed after a lot of struggles in the basin, it likely purchased the properties cheap from a lot of anxious sellers, similar to the situation with the San Juan Basin Royalty Trust. Right now, the situation looks really good. But this industry is cyclical. There will likely be more downcycles in the future. Oftentimes, these downcycles give little notice of the severity until it is really too late.

Risk

This is why it is highly recommended to watch something like this through a full business cycle before investing. Some of these turn out to have costs that are too high to make it through a downcycle with really any debt at all. Others pay out money that should have been used to repay debt, and then a downcycle hits (and they are gone for one reason or another).

Right now, the distribution is enticing, and cash flow looks good with the Iranian crisis. But no one knows how long the current situation will last. A wrong guess could prove to be very damaging to your portfolio or even fatal (because these are things that happened in the past).

The other consideration is that the low decline rate in this era of unconventional wells often means that the production is older. But that also means that lease operating costs will rise as production declines. Now there is some drilling activity that may keep costs down. But this is yet another good reason to wait for a full business cycle of results before investing.

Patience in this business is worth money. That is especially true for all of us who were writing about this type of setup back around 2015 only to see it fall apart over the next several years. Learn from all of us that got the “bumps and bruises” to show for this idea.

Summary

Mach Natural Resources LP is a K-1. Such a setup in the past has been found to have serious defects. In fact, many of those defects resulted in K-1s in the upstream business disappearing. That never stops others from trying (as is the case here). But since there have been so many failures in the past, it might well be best to wait to see if this particular setup makes it through a cyclical downturn (and in what condition).

This industry is very fast moving, volatile, and low visibility. What looks good when prices are reasonably high can quickly become untenable during a cyclical downturn. Because of that low visibility, cyclical downturns rarely advertise the severity and duration until it is too late.

For me, that makes this a sell consideration. I generally want a five-year history, at least, for any new issue before I consider it. In this industry, I prefer a full business cycle unless I know the management. With a K-1 model, as is the case here, I want to be especially careful before I consider investing. This setup does not have a good upstream track record.

data.title: State Of The Pipeline - March 2026
data.description:
This feature has the latest news from the mutual bank conversion sector. As of March 25, five conversions were in the pipeline.

Carteret, New Jersey-based URSB Bancorp Inc.'s (URSB) mutual-to-stock conversion offering priced at the supermax. The shares are expected to begin trading March 27.

According to the prospectus, many of the one- to four-family, multifamily, commercial and industrial (C&I), and consumer loans at URSB Bancorp unit United Roosevelt Savings Bank have been purchased rather than originated internally. As of Sept. 30, 2025, the balance of purchased C&I loans from BHG Financial, formerly known as Bankers Healthcare Group LLC, was $35.3 million, or 12.5% of the bank's loan portfolio.

United Roosevelt began buying C&I loans from BHG in early 2016. The bank also started buying participation interests in syndicated leveraged lending loans in November 2023 from BancAlliance Inc.; the balance of that exposure on Sept. 30, 2025, was $4.0 million. Additionally, United Roosevelt began purchasing consumer loans from BHG and Woodside Credit LLC in the second quarter of 2025 and from LendingClub Corp. (LC) in the first quarter of 2025.

On March 13, CSB Financial Inc. (CSB:PVT), the proposed holding company for Bethel, Ohio-based Community Savings Bank, filed a registration statement for a mutual-to-stock conversion. Community Savings began buying consumer loans from BHG in the second quarter of 2024. The consumer loan portfolio, virtually all of which came from BHG, comprised 18.7% of the bank's total loans as of year-end 2025. In the filing, the bank said, "We intend to continue to purchase unsecured consumer loans." The bank also disclosed that it has purchased C&I loans from BHG and that the majority of its one- to four-family loans are secured by nonowner-occupied properties.

Also on March 13, Poughkeepsie, New York-based Rhinebeck Bancorp Inc. (RBKB) filed a registration statement for a second-stage conversion. The exchange ratio for the newly issued shares is between 1.0367 at the minimum of the offering range and 1.4026 at the maximum. There is no supermax level. At the end of 2025, nonowner-occupied commercial real estate accounted for 27.3% of the bank's loan portfolio and indirect auto represented another 22.3%.

In the filing, Rhinebeck Bancorp said, "While we still plan to originate indirect automobile loans, over the past four years and for the foreseeable future we have actively decreased our indirect automobile portfolio by decreasing loan originations through increased pricing and more selective underwriting criteria."

Rhinebeck Bancorp also noted that it no longer originates residential mortgage loans directly and doesn't offer construction or rehabilitation loans for one- to four-family properties.

PSB Financial Inc. (PSB:PVT) has filed a prospectus for a mutual-to-stock conversion. PSB is the proposed holding company for Pioneer State Bank, the successor to Deer Lodge, Montana-based Pioneer Federal Savings and Loan Association. The price to pro forma tangible book value as of Dec. 31, 2025, was between 41.5% at the minimum of the offering range and 53.7% at the supermax. The closing date for the offering is April 21.

In the filing, PSB said, "We are considering branching opportunities that may arise in our primary market area, including a potential branch office in western Montana in late 2026 or the first six months of 2027."

On Feb. 2, Fair Lawn, New Jersey-based Columbia Financial Inc. announced a relatively rare type of transaction: a mutual bank conversion plus a bank acquisition. Columbia's announcement was for a second-stage conversion and the simultaneous acquisition of Woodbridge, New Jersey-based Northfield Bancorp Inc.

Then on March 6, Columbia filed its conversion registration statement. The exchange ratio ranges from 1.8729 at the adjusted minimum of the offering range to 2.534 at the maximum. As with Rhinebeck Bancorp, there is no supermax. Following the transaction, Columbia intends to pay a quarterly cash dividend of 5 cents per share.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GitLab: Growth Is Slowing, But Cheap Enough To Keep Buying
data.description:
As we move deeper into 2026, selling pressure continues to build against software stocks. Investors who were used to having upward multiples re-ratings drive the majority of stock gains over the past few years are now seeing the unfortunate inverse. And because it’s difficult to ascertain the impacts of AI on sales, we may be looking at a multi-quarter or even multi-year rut in the sector.

Amid these conditions, however, investors with long time horizons have a great chance to buy high-quality businesses at a sharp discount. GitLab (GTLB) is one of these stocks. The DevOps software platform, which helps companies build and deploy software applications, has seen a ~40% reduction in its share price since the start of the year and a ~60% loss in the past twelve months. The question for investors now is, will the bleeding continue, or is it time to step in?

I last wrote a buy article on GitLab in December, when the stock was trading at $40 per share. I fully acknowledge that the timing of my buy call was ill-timed, and needless to say, I wasn’t able to predict that the “SaaSpocalypse” narrative would overtake markets in 2026 and sink GitLab further. That said, with a decline of this magnitude, it helps to take a step back and look at the stock with fresh eyes. Yes, it’s true that GitLab’s growth is decelerating. But there is little evidence to substantiate the notion that AI will entirely kill its business. With that in mind, I’m reiterating my buy call on this stock.

Let’s start with the elephant in the room that has tanked GitLab’s stock: its guidance for FY27. For the year ending in January 2027, as shown in the chart below, GitLab has guided to $1.10-$1.12 billion in revenue, which reflects 15-17% y/y growth. This represents a deceleration from 23% growth in Q3, and unfortunately does little to defray the fear that AI is killing off GitLab’s market. Further, the company is expecting pro forma operating income to fall -18% y/y, with pro forma operating margins decaying to 12% relative to a 17% margin in FY26.

We won’t argue that these metrics aren’t a concern. GitLab has a lot of work ahead of it to reset its sales traction and continue expanding its customer base. Yet there are glimmers of hope: the company notes that recent new logo adds have rebounded. It’s possible that guidance is setting the stage for a low bar to cross, with beat-and-raise quarters ahead of us in FY27.

The upshot here that we need to consider is that this weakness is already priced into GitLab’s stock. At current share prices near $21, GitLab trades at a market cap of $3.46 billion. After we net off the $1.26 billion of cash (against zero debt) on GitLab’s most recent balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $2.20 billion.

This represents a 2.0x EV/FY27 revenue multiple. It’s true here that we need to take GitLab’s execution on a bit of faith: investors are demanding that software stocks be reasonably valued against the bottom line, and with the company expecting operating margin erosion this year, it’s difficult to take that for granted. That said, we shouldn’t ignore the fact that GitLab trades well below most software companies that are growing at a similar mid-teens pace:

While we think it may take several quarters for investors to get comfortable with GitLab again (we'll need to see proof of strong execution and GitLab maintaining at least mid/high teens revenue growth), the company should be able to recover its multiples closer in line with peers: at least in a 3-4x revenue multiple range.

Furthermore, consider the nature of the AI “risk” for GitLab. It’s true that vibe coding and AI may reduce the need for software engineers whose principal job is to write code. But we’re far away from a future (and perhaps will never achieve) in which AI generates usable, shippable code in the first iteration without the need for human review, revision, and ultimately support and maintenance. It is platforms like GitLab that enable this type of work: so as long as the company can decouple its business model from seat-based licenses (which it already has done) and base it on consumption, GitLab is likely never to become obsolete.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let’s now go through GitLab's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

GitLab's revenue grew 23% y/y to $260.4 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $252.2 million. Revenue growth decelerated two points from 25% y/y growth in Q3.

Again, we do think that GitLab's growth slowdown is worth noting. That said, it's not all bad news for the company either. GitLab grew its total base customer count to 10,682, adding 207 net-new customers within the quarter. Moreover, the count of customers generating >$1 million in ARR grew 26% y/y to 155 customers.

We wouldn't be seeing this type of customer activity if GitLab was a platform at near-term risk of being decommissioned. The fact that larger enterprise customers are committing to heavy spending with GitLab is a healthy signal of the company's durability.

And we note that while management has cited disappointment with recent sales trends, its recent trend of logo additions has been on the rise. The company has also added to its salesforce, which may drive a near-term hit to margins but sets the company up for growth recovery as tenures improve. Per CEO Bill Staples' remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

FY '27 is all about execution and proving our hypothesis with results. Let me walk through each one. In FY '26, we reversed a long period of first order deceleration. This is critically important because our customers often land small but extend steadily, a pattern that's held for over a decade. Sales-led first orders began reaccelerating in Q2 FY '26 right after Ian joined. On the product side, Manav has reinvigorated product-led growth.

First order logos inflected in October, and we've seen 4 consecutive months of improvement. For FY '27, we now see a clear path to sustained acceleration in first orders, driven by continued sign-up momentum, new product-led on-ramps and a dedicated first order sales team with a new global leader, 4 regional leads in place and rapid hiring underway. In fact, the team has already closed their first deals in Q1 [...]

With regard to sales capacity, we began increasing headcount in FY '26, and we're entering FY '27 with more capacity than we've ever had. And we have a path to even stronger ramped capacity beginning in Q3. With FY '27 kickoff, we've overhauled territory design to also better serve all segments and strengthen enablement."

Management noted as well that gross retention is consistent with historical trends, while churn has hit a 4-year low: all additional signals that AI isn't driving any near-term crisis for GitLab.

For now, profitability is also expanding. Pro forma operating margins rose 3 points y/y to 21%:

The company's guidance calling for margin erosion in FY27 is a reflection of the company's recent step-up in sales hiring, but if new logo additions continue to outpace expectations, we expect the company to be able to "beat and raise" above its current bar.

Risks and key takeaways

It's true that GitLab has faced an elevated amount of execution risk lately. We should be cognizant of the risks that sit on the near-term horizon for the company. As a weak macro persists and puts pressure on IT budgets, we have evidence of companies putting less budget priority on DevOps tools like GitLab while devoting IT spending to projects that directly enable AI. We note as well that GitLab operates in a competitive environment, and it has a number of notable direct competitors like JFrog (FROG) that has seen better recent sales execution.

At the same time, it's far too premature to call out AI as an existential threat to GitLab. It's still growing its large customer counts, is growing revenue at a >20% pace, and at least as of Q4 still retained a "Rule of 40" growth/profitability balance. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Hermès: In An AI World Flooded With Abundance, Scarcity May Become Even More Valuable
data.description:
I wrote about Hermès (HESAY) (HESAF) previously. At the time, I saw it as a wonderful brand, even a model for luxury brands, carrying fantastic financial performance. Unfortunately, the valuation seemed off at the time, and at $235 per share, I thought it only deserved a Hold rating, and I even added that I would consider it at $190. The gentleman below made a very important note:

If you are waiting for a stock of this caliber to trade cheap, you are in for a hard time. And he was right; only two years later has the stock hit the level I had defined, and I must say it was more luck than merit.

In my view, the only reason the brand reached this level again is mainly due to an awful macro tape, where the closing of the Strait of Hormuz is akin to a tax on global productivity, which makes Hermès goods relatively less valuable (declining output of the rest of the economy), and the market is just discounting it. But in the end I believe the story is still about manufactured scarcity and the AI productivity boom that ultimately strengthens, not weakens, the Hermès thesis.

Recent performance

The recent selloff in the stock seems more macro than micro, given that the company has entered 2026 from a strong position. In 2025, the company recorded close to $18 billion in revenue, with an operating margin of 41.75%. It should be noted that this is a very strong combination in any consumer category, let alone a year marked by tariff uncertainty and currency pressure.

The balance sheet is also in great shape with a current ratio of almost 5 and a debt-to-assets ratio below 10%, confirming modest debt usage and plenty of liquidity available for the company.

The leather goods and saddlery are still the most important segment for the company’s business, and they grew at 13.1% in 2025. The cash generation is impressive, with the company recording close to $5 billion in 2025.

This gives the companies multiple options, although the path ahead seems to be the continuation of the cautious strategy of reinvesting in the controlled scarcity model through the slow ramp of new workshops in a way that won’t dilute the brand while retaining ample liquidity to cushion macro cycles without having to resort to layoffs. This last point is key given that the company needs a lot of time to train new artisans, and it makes no sense to train them to let them go because of cyclical fluctuation in business.

All-in-all, if the closing of the Strait of Hormuz is indeed the catalyst for a global downturn, Hermès appears to be in a good position to cushion this without having to resort to short-term measures and eventually throw the proverbial baby out with the bathwater.

Valuation

A look into valuation allows us to confirm that Hermès is indeed a different breed. Even with the recent drawdown, the stock remains a clear outlier on sales multiple. At 11x sales, it sits well above all the other companies. The market continues to price Hermès as a scarcity asset with fantastic economics.

This actually supports our assumption that the drawdown was macro-driven, since the premium has remained. The market decided to mark down the stock since the “global tax on global productivity” squeezes the surplus that finances discretionary spending. But, still, it wasn’t willing to value the company as an ordinary luxury company.

On the P/E multiple, the story is similar, even if there are some nuances. For starters, the sales multiple seems much more extreme than the PE. Usually that happens when the market is not just rewarding current profit margins but the whole structure of the business: we mean the brand power, the scarcity manufacturing model, direct distribution, and production bottleneck. All these features mean that future growth might be available at the expense of no luxury brand dilution.

Risks

There are a couple of risks that can attack the earnings, multiple or the scarcity thesis. Let’s review them. The present macro shock can compress the multiple. As we have framed it, this is a global tax on productivity that trickles down, reducing the surplus for high-end discretionary spending. Also, in these situations the market can become indiscriminate, and everything gets sold at the same time. The stock can go down without the business breaking.

China and Asia represented 42% of the revenue, and it is by far the biggest revenue exposure. The current political tensions or even problems in the Chinese economy can turn into a problem for Hermès.

Probably the most important thesis-breaking risk would be if production volume suddenly starts to outrun the consumer perception of the brand’s scarcity. The whole idea in this company is to scale without becoming common. If there are troubles, the first warning will come from the resale market losing steam. That alone can trigger a violent re-rating.

Wrapping up

Hermès is a rare publicly traded scarcity asset. The recent drawdown is likely motivated by macro worries that compress the global surplus from which discretionary spending (including luxury spending) is funded. Nevertheless, the long term revenue engine remains intact with $18 billion in revenues in 2025, and a 41% operating margin, providing the clout to advance the scaling of scarcity without cheapening it. In a world on the cusp of being flooded with synthetic abundance from AI, human-made capacity constrained physical objects may become relatively more valuable, not less.

An interesting way to wrap this up is to build a bull and bear scenario. For the bull scenario, let’s consider the revenue estimates for 2028, with the same multiple and constant share count. In that case the equity value would be 11.2 times $25.16 billion (high end of analysts estimates), or $282 billion. Considering 105 million ordinary shares (10 ADR for 1 ordinary share in Euronext Paris Exchange), means a target of $268.

For the bear scenario, let’s consider the low end of the 2028 estimates, $22.86 billion at 7.1x revenue (Ferrari multiple), which results in an implied equity value of $162 billion, or $154 per share. Given the current price of $188, the bull case implies an annual return of close to 13%, while the bear case provides a return of -6%.

All in all, the risk/return relation is positive even if the overall potential seems limited. I loved the business model back in 2024, and I love the business even more today. The stock price dipping is a gift, not a problem, in my perspective. I think it deserves a place in any long term-oriented investor, therefore I am rating it a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gilead Sciences: Caution After A Re-Rating Amidst New Concentration Risks
data.description:
Shares of Gilead Sciences (GILD) have been on a major run higher in recent years, far outperforming many peers that have been struggling. This made sense, as Gilead traded at very low valuations from the get-go.

The problem is that this move higher has been driven by modest growth, yet amidst a re-rating in the valuation and Gilead rapidly getting reliant on its HIV business, I am much more cautious here. This compares to a much more bullish outlook in the fall of 2024. A recent M&A streak is not going to change that cautious stance here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent IPOs, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Continued M&A

Gilead announced its second >$1 billion deal before the first quarter of the year had ended. Towards the end of March, Gilead announced a $1.675 billion cash deal to acquire Ouro Medicines, a privately held biotechnology company that focuses on developing T cell engager therapies for autoimmune diseases.

With this deal, the company will get its hands on OM366 (gamgertamig), which has shown great efficacy in phase 1/2 clinical studies in antibody-mediated orphan diseases, with the FDA having granted fast-track and orphan drug designation. The deal involves another half a billion in contingent milestone payments, but all this could change a bit as Gilead is in discussions with Galapagos to effectively "share" the deal, as both firms could pay up 50% in this transaction.

About a month ago, Gilead announced a much larger $7.8 billion deal to acquire Arcellx (ACLX) in a $115 per share cash deal, excluding a potential $5 per share earn-out through a contingent value consideration. Arcellx has multiple drugs in its pipeline; the most promising is a CAR T-cell therapy for multiple myeloma.

With the company spending up to $10 billion in M&A, that was not all, as the company hiked the dividend in February. Following a modest near 4% increase in the payout, these payouts now come in at $0.82 per share each quarter, at an expense of about $4 billion per year.

On A Stronger Footing

Gilead announced solid 2025 results early in February. Reported revenues rose 2% to $29.4 billion. However, underlying growth came in at 4%, with regular product revenue reported at $28.0 billion; that is, if we exclude Veklury (the COVID-19 drug of Gilead), whose revenues were cut in half to less than a billion in 2025.

The problem, or challenge, for Gilead is that the company is creating a reliance issue again. The HIV business grew sales by 6% to $20.8 billion, making up nearly three-quarters of product revenue. Liver disease revenues grew 6% to $3.2 billion, this once being a cornerstone of the initial wave of success of Gilead, which now has been stabilized. Oncology sales were actually down 2% to $3.2 billion, with other revenues coming in below the billion mark.

Earnings are quite lumpy, with adjusted earnings of $8.15 per share for 2025 being nearly twice the number a year before, with a substantial part of the volatility being the result of volatile IPR&D impairment charges, depressing 2024's earnings to a large degree.

Paying for the recent M&A streak is relatively easy, as a net debt load of $17 billion and change has come down to $14 billion over the past year. While this will jump to $24 billion on a pro forma basis, such debt levels are very manageable for a firm the size and profitability of Gilead here. This comes as the 2025 guidance calls for a continuation of modest growth, with product sales excluding Veklury seen up just over a billion to $29.0-$29.4 billion. Adjusted earnings are seen at a midpoint of $8.65 per share, up half a dollar from the year before.

With 1.25 billion shares outstanding trading at $137 per share, the company commands a market value of $171 billion and a $185 billion enterprise valuation ahead of the recent deals. This values the business at just over 6 times sales, and based on the adjusted earnings guidance, valuations come in at around 17 times trailing earnings and 16 times forward earnings.

This marks a huge re-rating for a stock that traded around 10 times earnings many times in recent years. Such observation comes even after shares are down twenty dollars from the highs a couple of weeks ago, as shares have still doubled from a $70 mark in the summer of 2024.

What Now?

While the re-rating has been substantial and adjusted earnings in the case of pharmaceutical companies can be quite adjusted, I fear the composition of growth of Gilead. After all, following a transformative acquisition of Pharmasset, Gilead became a hugely successful business, thriving on its HCV business in the 2010s. Note that Gilead at large is still reporting sales way short compared to the sales peak around that time, induced by the HCV boom.

With the company rapidly curing these patients, the fortunes of the business turned as the company was in desperate need of new blockbusters. To that end, the company used a lot of capital to pursue M&A, on average to relatively little avail. This included a $21 billion deal to acquire Immunomedics, a $12 billion deal for Kite Pharma, a $4 billion deal for CymaBay, multi-billion investments in Galapagos, all ahead of recent M&A efforts here and smaller deals announced meanwhile.

Meanwhile, absent some volatility induced by the COVID-19 drug Veklury, the share of HIV has been increasing rapidly as the HCV business imploded. That was not all, as HIV grew while other segments were seen coming down, despite their M&A efforts. All of this makes it so that the share of HIV product sales has risen to nearly three-quarters of product sales here, making me very cautious in between more demanding valuations and more concentrated risks.

Having held a position in Gilead for a long period of time, I collected relatively fat dividends as I have been gradually trimming the position on the move higher. By now, I have sold out of the last shares around the $130 mark.

This decision was driven by re-rating and the dependency risks created here, and while the company has seen shares come down a bit recently, that follows strong momentum to the upside before as well. However, their recent M&A efforts are encouraging, but frankly, it is very difficult to see how these deals will pan out and how product revenues will evolve, as that remains wait-and-see. Growth in such acquired, or internally developed drugs is key to changing the narrative here, as the future will tell if, how fast, and how large this will happen.

That will be key, as seeing appeal at such multiples requires more growth and more diversified growth, with few signs seen at this point, making it easy for me to avoid the shares here.

data.title: Why Active Bond Management Matters When Spreads Are Tight
data.description:
Corporate bonds delivered a strong 2025. The broad investment-grade market posted a total return of approximately 7.77%, rewarding investors who maintained fixed income allocations through a volatile rate environment.1 More than $540 billion flowed into taxable bond funds over the course of the year, the highest annual total on record.2

That enthusiasm, however, has compressed credit spreads to levels not seen in decades. Investment-grade option-adjusted spreads entered 2026 in the 2nd percentile of their 20-year range, leaving little room for further tightening.3 The question facing bond investors now is straightforward: in an environment where the easy gains from spread compression may already be behind us, where does income come from next?

Tight Spreads Demand a Different Approach

When spreads are wide, nearly any diversified bond portfolio benefits from the rising tide. When spreads are historically tight, the margin for error shrinks. A single credit misstep can erase months of carry. This is the environment bond investors face in 2026.

Analysts at Aberdeen Investments have noted that security selection becomes more important than simply owning the market when spreads offer limited compensation for credit risk.3

Charles Schwab has echoed a cautiously constructive view, calling 2026 “another generally good year for bonds” while emphasizing the importance of selectivity.4 Corporate bond supply may reach a record $2.25 trillion in gross issuance this year, which means investors will face no shortage of new paper to evaluate.5

Investment-grade credit spreads have compressed steadily since the 2022 peak and now sit near the tightest levels observed in two decades, reducing the passive beta opportunity in corporate bonds. Basis Points (bps). Beta - in finance measures an investment’s volatility or systematic risk compared to the overall market (usually the S&P 500), acting as a gauge for sensitivity.

An Active Approach to Corporate Bond Income

The Infrastructure Capital Bond Income ETF (BNDS) is built for precisely this kind of market. Launched in January 2025, the fund is actively managed and holds a focused portfolio of approximately 73 corporate bonds selected through a combination of quantitative and qualitative analysis.6 The management team emphasizes securities it believes are undervalued on the basis of term premium, credit premium, and liquidity premium.

BNDS currently offers a 30-day SEC yield of 7.67%, well above the yields available from broad market benchmarks such as iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) or Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (BND).6 Monthly distributions of $0.34 per share, recently increased in December 2025, underscore the fund’s income-first mandate.

Top holdings reflect a preference for mid-tier corporate credits in energy infrastructure and real estate, including names like Genesis Energy, Chemours, Plains All American Pipeline, Sunoco LP, and BW Real Estate.6 This is not a fund that mirrors the index. The concentrated, conviction-driven positioning is a deliberate feature of the active strategy. *Fund holdings are subject to change at any time and should not be considered a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Click here for the fund’s Top Ten Holdings.

Taxable bond fund inflows surged past $540 billion in 2025, with active strategies capturing a growing share of total flows. Two-thirds of bond ETFs created since 2019 are actively managed. Data obtained from Morningstar.

What This Means for Income Investors

The broader shift toward active fixed income management is well documented. Active bond ETFs captured more than $100 billion in flows through September 2025 alone, representing roughly 40% of all fixed income ETF inflows.2 That trend reflects a growing recognition that index-hugging strategies may leave income on the table, particularly when the spread environment offers limited passive tailwinds.

With the Federal Reserve holding the fed funds rate at 3.50%–3.75% and markets pricing further easing toward 3.00%–3.25% over the course of 2026, the backdrop for bond returns remains constructive.7

The opportunity, though, increasingly lies in careful credit work and disciplined security selection rather than broad market exposure. Funds like BNDS, with an expense ratio of 0.81% and a transparent, income-focused mandate, represent one way investors can pursue that opportunity.6

Tight spreads do not necessarily mean poor returns. They mean the returns must come from somewhere other than passive spread compression. Active management, selective credit exposure, and income enhancement strategies offer a path forward for investors willing to look beyond the index.

Current yield comparison

BNDS offers a 30-day yield of 7.67% as of 12/31/2025, significantly above the yields available from broad bond benchmarks (AGG, BND) and the 10-year Treasury. Performance data quoted represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end performance, please call (800) 617-0004 or visit the fund's website at https://www.infracapfund.com/BNDS.

Notes

1. Breckinridge Capital Advisors, Q1 2026 Corporate Bond Market Outlook, January 2026.

2. Morningstar, 8 Charts on U.S. Fund Flows in 2025, January 2026.

3. Breckinridge Capital Advisors, Q1 2026 Corporate Bond Market Outlook, January 2026; Aberdeen Investments, Global Fixed Income Outlook 2026, December 2025.

4. Charles Schwab, The Bond Market in 2026: What Could Go Wrong?, January 2026.

5. Morgan Stanley, 2026 U.S. Credit Strategy Outlook, November 2025.

6. Infrastructure Capital Advisors, BNDS Fund Overview and Prospectus, 2025.

7. Federal Reserve Board, FOMC Statement, January 28, 2026.

Disclosure

ICA will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information.
